q3: The model kept treating structure as something to fix reactively — I'd point out that a section needed subsections and it would add them, but it never anticipated that a multi-stage flow needs that treatment upfront. The 15-line code limit also kept slipping; it would get the function under the limit after feedback but the first draft would almost always come in long.
q5: I looked at it, and it captured the right things — the rejected-alternatives pattern, the code discipline, warnings before the section rather than after. Those are exactly what I care about. But the rubric didn't seem to stop the model from making the same mistakes on first drafts; it felt more like the rubric helped the model recover faster when I pushed back than it helped the model get it right the first time.
q6: The rubric made it clearer that the model's default is to front-load explanation in prose but then let code examples carry conceptual weight anyway — it would write good framing paragraphs and then include inline comments that were doing work the prose should have done. I probably would have noticed this eventually, but seeing the 'prose-code weight distribution' criterion named explicitly made the pattern obvious faster.

q3: The code example discipline was the persistent struggle — the model kept trying to show too much in a single snippet, conflating multiple concerns into one block instead of isolating the pattern the prose had already explained. I had to explicitly call this out before it clicked.
q5: I looked at it, yes. The rubric captured most of what I care about — the rejected-alternatives framing and the prose-before-code ordering both map directly to things I actually want. What it doesn't capture well is the narrative header requirement; 'Narrative Coherence' is vague enough that the model could satisfy it with labeled sections rather than headers that tell a story.
q6: The rubric made it clearer that the model's default is to treat code examples as the primary explanation vehicle and use prose as secondary — which is the opposite of how I want it. Without the rubric I might have just thought the examples were individually too long; with it I could see the model was consistently under-investing in prose as the conceptual front-load.

q3: The main recurring issue was code examples trying to do too much at once — conflating multiple concerns into a single snippet instead of narrowing to the one pattern the prose was setting up. The model generally fixed this when I flagged it, but it kept happening on first drafts.
q5: I looked at it, and it maps reasonably well to what I actually care about — the rejected-alternatives framing and the prose-before-code weight distribution are both real priorities for me. That said, the rubric phrases things in a way that's a bit more mechanical than how I think about it; I don't consciously score 'Design Decision Reasoning' as priority one, I just get frustrated when an architectural choice is stated without context.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model was actually pretty consistent about naming rejected alternatives — that part it had internalized well by iteration 4. What it surfaced is that the code discipline criterion was the weak spot; the rubric says 'stay at or under ~15 lines' but the model still reached for comprehensive examples on first pass, which tells me the rubric constraint wasn't being weighted heavily enough in practice.

q3: The main recurring issue was code example scope — the model kept trying to show too much in a single snippet, conflating multiple concerns that should be separate. It took a few rounds of feedback to get examples down to the minimal runnable slice I actually wanted. The heading comment discipline also needed nudging more than once.
q5: I looked at it, yes. The rubric captured the rejected-alternative reasoning well — that's priority #1 and it showed in the drafts, which consistently named alternatives and explained the tradeoffs concretely. The code example discipline criteria were also aligned with what I care about, though the model still needed feedback to actually hit the line count target. What the rubric missed was the narrative header convention — 'what's happening at each stage' is close but it didn't prevent the model from occasionally defaulting to label-style headers before I pushed back.
q6: The rubric made it clearer that the model's default is to over-explain in inline comments rather than letting the prose do that work — it kept reaching for comments as a shortcut when the surrounding text hadn't established the concept yet. That pattern was there in previous iterations but the rubric's prose-code weight criteria made me notice it as a consistent habit rather than a one-off.

q3: The atomicity warning placement kept slipping — it would drift back toward the end of sections even after I explicitly asked for it upfront. The model also had a recurring tendency to write heading comments that described the first operation in a code block rather than the block's overall purpose, which I had to correct more than once.
q5: I looked at it, yes. The rubric captured the rejected-alternative reasoning and the code comment discipline pretty accurately — those two criteria map closely to things I actually care about. What it didn't fully capture is the 'warnings before, not after' placement rule, which I think is why the atomicity issue kept recurring even with the rubric in play.
q6: The rubric made it clearer that the model's default is to treat warnings as addenda — it consistently placed them after the relevant section as if they were footnotes rather than prerequisites. I probably would have noticed this pattern eventually, but seeing the rubric's criteria made it obvious the model was satisfying the letter of 'include warnings' without satisfying the spirit of 'place them where they prevent the problem.'
